name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعًا (١٩)
```

```english
Indeed man has been created covetous: (19)
```

```arabic
إِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ جَزُوعًا (٢٠)
```

```english
anxious when an ill befalls him (20)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلْخَيْرُ مَنُوعًا (٢١)
```

```english
and grudging [charity] when good comes his way (21)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٢٢)
```

```english
—[all are such] except the prayerful, (22)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ دَآئِمُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
those who persevere in their prayers (23)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَّعْلُومٌ (٢٤)
```

```english
and there is a known share in whose wealth (24)
```

```arabic
لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (٢٥)
```

```english
for the beggar and the deprived, (25)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٢٦)
```

```english
and who affirm the Day of Retribution, (26)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ (٢٧)
```

```english
and those who are apprehensive of the punishment of their Lord (27)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ (٢٨)
```

```english
(there is indeed no security from the punishment of their Lord) (28)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَـٰفِظُونَ (٢٩)
```

```english
and those who guard their private parts (29)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَزْوَٰجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ (٣٠)
```

```english
(apart from their spouses and their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (30)
```

```arabic
فَمَنِ ٱبْتَغَىٰ وَرَآءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْعَادُونَ (٣١)
```

```english
but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors) (31)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَمَـٰنَـٰتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ رَٰعُونَ (٣٢)
```

```english
and those who keep their trusts and covenants, (32)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُم بِشَهَـٰدَٰتِهِمْ قَآئِمُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
and those who are observant of their testimonies, (33)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلَىٰ صَلَاتِهِمْ يُحَافِظُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
and those who are watchful of their prayers. (34)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ مُّكْرَمُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
They will be in gardens, held in honour. (35)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses point toward the primary cause that incites man toward the vices of turning away, fleeing, hoarding, and withholding—actions that lead him to enter the eternal Fire, which is a raging flame (لظى) that strips away the scalp (نزاعة للشوى), as mentioned in the verses.

That cause is the trait of anxious-greed (الهلع) — *the intense innate drive for acquisition and self-preservation* — which Divine Wisdom necessitated that man be created upon, so that he might be guided by it toward what contains his goodness and happiness. However, man corrupts this trait within himself and misuses it in the pursuit of his happiness, such that it leads him to perpetual destruction—except for those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات), for they are in gardens, held in high honor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ خُلِقَ هَلُوعاً إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً** **"Indeed man was created anxious-greedy: (19) impatient when ill befalls him, (20) and grudging when good befalls him"** (70:19-21). The term "anxious-greedy" (الهلوع) is an adjective derived from *al-hala‘* (الهلع), which denotes intense greed or covetousness. It has also been mentioned that the term is explained by the two verses following it; thus, he is "impatient" (الجزوع) when evil touches him and "grudging/withholding" (المنوع) when good touches him. This is a sound explanation and fits the context.

This is because the intense greed (الحرص الشديد) upon which man is naturally disposed (جبل عليه) is not a greed for everything—whether good or evil, beneficial or harmful—but rather a greed for what is good and beneficial. Furthermore, it is not a greed for every good or benefit regardless of whether it relates to him or not, or whether it belongs to him or another; rather, it is his greed for what he perceives as good for himself or beneficial in the path of goodness. A necessary consequence of this greed is that instability and agitation appear in him when "evil" (الشر)—which is the opposite of good—touches him, and that he refuses to let go of "good" (الخير) when he possesses it, prioritizing himself over others unless he perceives that letting go is better and more beneficial for his state. Thus, impatience (الجزع) when evil touches and withholding (المنع) when good touches are among the necessary concomitants of anxious-greed (الهلع) and intense greed.

Anxious-greed (الهلع) and intense greed (الحرص الشديد) upon which man is naturally disposed—which is a branch of self-love (حب الذات)—is not, in itself, a blameworthy vice. How could it be, when it is the sole means that impels man toward attaining his happiness and the perfection of his existence? It only becomes a blameworthy vice if man mismanages it.

Thus, he employs it where he should and where he should not, rightfully and wrongfully, just like all other psychological traits (الصفات النفسانية) which are noble as long as they adhere to the limit of moderation (الاعتدال), but when they deviate toward the side of excess (الإفراط) or deficiency (التفريط), they turn into a despicable vice.

In the beginning of his development, while he is a child, man perceives what he considers good for himself or evil for himself through the emotional instincts (الغرائز العاطفة) with which he has been equipped. These are what his soul desires and his faculties crave, without him limiting them by any boundary or measuring them by any standard. Thus, he becomes impatient (يجزع) if pain or any disliked thing touches him, and he withholds (يمنع) from whoever competes with him for what he holds with all his power, such as crying and the like.

He remains in this state until he is granted intellect (العقل) and maturity (الرشد), at which point he perceives truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), and good (الخير) and evil (الشر). His soul acknowledges what he has perceived, and at that moment, many of the instances (مصاديق) of truth, falsehood, good, and evil change for him. Much of what he used to see as good for himself becomes evil in his view, and vice versa.

If he persists in what he was upon—following the whims of the soul (أهواء النفس) and being devoted to its cravings, becoming occupied with them away from following the truth and being heedless of it—his heart becomes sealed. He then encounters no truth except that he refutes it, and no possessor of a right except that he oppresses him. But if Divine Care (العناية الإلهية) reaches him, that greed he had for what the soul desires turns into a greed for the truth. Thus, he does not act arrogantly toward a truth he encounters, nor does he withhold from a possessor of a right his due.

So man, in his initial state—the period of childhood before reaching maturity (البلوغ) and discernment (الرشد)—is equipped with intense greed for good, which is a trait of perfection (صفة كمالية) for him according to his state, by which he is motivated to seek good and avoid evil. The Exalted said: **وَإِنَّهُ لِحُبِّ الْخَيْرِ لَشَدِيدٌ** **"and indeed he is passionate in his love of good"** (100:8).

Then, when he is granted maturity and discernment, he is increased with another equipment, which is the intellect (العقل) — *the faculty of discernment that perceives the realities of things as they truly are*. Through it, he perceives what constitutes true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and what constitutes goodness in action. His intense greed for "good" and his being impatient when evil touches and withholding when good touches are transformed from greed for worldly good into intense greed for the actual good (الخير الواقعي). This manifests as anguish and fear when an otherworldly evil (شر أخروي) touches him—which is sin (المعصية)—and racing toward the forgiveness of his Lord when an otherworldly good (خير أخروي) touches him—which is encountering a virtuous deed (الحسنة). As for worldly evil and good, he does not exceed in them what Allah has bounded for him of patience (الصبر) during calamity, patience in obedience, and patience against sin. This trait is a trait of perfection for this human being.

However, if man turns away from what his intellect perceives and his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — acknowledges, and devotes himself to following whims (الهوى), embracing falsehood, and encroaching upon the right of every possessor of a right, and does not stop his greed for good at any limit—

Then he has exchanged the blessing of Allah for a curse. He has taken an instinctive trait (صفة غريزية) that Allah created as a means for him to reach the happiness of this world and the Hereafter, and turned it into a means toward wretchedness (الشقوة) and destruction (الهلكة), driving him toward turning away, fleeing, hoarding, and withholding, as mentioned in the verses.

It has become clear from what preceded that there is no harm in attributing man's anxious-greed (هلع) in the verses to creation (الخلقة), even though the speech is intended for blame. The Exalted has said: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** **"who perfected everything He created"** (32:7). This is because the blame that attaches to it is only from the side of man and his mismanagement, not from His side, the Exalted. It is like all His other blessings upon man, which he turns into curses through his poor choice (سوء اختياره).

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned, in an attempt to escape this difficulty, that there is a metaphor (استعارة) in the speech. The meaning is that man, due to his preference for impatience and withholding and their firm rootedness in him, is as if he were naturally disposed and imprinted upon them, and as if it were a created matter within him that is necessary and non-voluntary. Thus, the speech is based on a comparison (التشبيه), not to indicate that it is truly created by Allah, because the speech is intended for blame, and Allah, the Glorified, does not blame His own act. Among the proofs for this is the exception of the believers who struggled against their souls and were saved from both impatience and withholding.

The response to this is that the trait is created as a blessing and a virtue, and it is man who removes it from the realm of virtue to vice, and from blessing to curse. The blame returns to the trait from the aspect of his mismanagement, not from the aspect of it being His act, the Exalted.

The exception of the believers is not because the trait is not created within them, but because they kept it upon its perfection and did not exchange it for vice and curse.

It has also been answered regarding the exception that it is a "disconnected exception" (منقطع), but this is as you can see [weak].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ** **"except the prayerful"** (70:22). This is an exception from "man" described with anxious-greed. In the precedence given to prayer (الصلاة) over the other righteous deeds enumerated in the following verses, there is an indication of its nobility and that it is the best of deeds.

Furthermore, it has a prominent effect in repelling the blameworthy vice of anxious-greed. The Exalted has said: **إِنَّ الصَّلاةَ تَنْهى عَنِ الْفَحْشاءِ وَالْمُنْكَرِ** **"Indeed the prayer prevents indecencies and wrongs"** (29:45).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ** **"those who are persevering in their prayers"** (70:23). In the annexation (إضافة) of prayer to the pronoun, there is an indication that they are constant in the prayers they perform, whatever they may be, not that they are always in a state of prayer. In this, there is an indication that an action only completes its effect through constancy (المداومة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ** **"and those in whose wealth there is a known right (24) for the beggar and the deprived"** (70:24-25). Some have interpreted it as the obligatory alms (الزكاة المفروضة). However, in the hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "The known right (الحق المعلوم) is not from the *zakat*—rather, it is a known amount they spend for the poor." The "beggar" (السائل) is the poor person who asks, and the "deprived" (المحروم) is the poor person who remains chaste and does not ask. The context is not devoid of supporting this, for the *zakat* has specific categories named in His saying: **إِنَّمَا الصَّدَقاتُ لِلْفُقَراءِ وَالْمَساكِينِ وَالْعامِلِينَ عَلَيْها وَالْمُؤَلَّفَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَفِي الرِّقابِ وَالْغارِمِينَ وَفِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ فَرِيضَةً مِنَ اللهِ** **"The charities are only for the poor and the needy, and those employed to collect them, and those whose hearts are to be reconciled, and for [the freedom of] the slaves and the debtors, and in the way of Allah, and for the traveller. [This is] an ordinance from Allah"** (9:60), and it is not restricted to the beggar and the deprived as is apparent in the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ يُصَدِّقُونَ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ** **"and those who confirm the Day of Retribution"** (70:26). What the context of enumerating righteous deeds suggests is that the meaning of their "confirming" (تصديقهم) the Day of Retribution is practical confirmation (التصديق العملي) rather than just creedal confirmation (التصديق الاعتقادي). This is such that their conduct in life is the conduct of one who sees that whatever action he performs, he will be held accountable for it and recompensed for it—if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

In the expression **يُصَدِّقُونَ** (they confirm), there is an indication of continuity (الاستمرار); thus, it is the constant self-vigilance (المراقبة الدائمة) by remembering Him, the Exalted, at every action they face, such that they perform what He desires and abandon what He dislikes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقُونَ** **"and those who are fearful of the punishment of their Lord"** (70:27). That is, they are afraid. The discussion regarding their fear (إشفاقهم) of the punishment of their Lord is similar to the discussion regarding their confirmation of the Day of Retribution; it is the practical fear (الإشفاق العملي) apparent from their state.

A necessary consequence of their fear of the punishment of their Lord—alongside their adherence to righteous deeds and their striving for the sake of Allah—is that they do not place absolute reliance in the righteous deeds they perform, nor do they feel secure (يأمنوا) from the punishment of Allah, for security (الأمن) does not coexist with fear.

The criterion for fear of the punishment is that punishment is for opposition; thus, there is no salvation from it except through the soul's obedience. Yet there is no reliance upon the soul, as it has no power in its essence except what Allah has empowered it with, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner (المالك) who is not owned. He, the Exalted, said: **قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً** **"Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah’"** (5:17).

Furthermore, although Allah, the Glorified, has promised the people of obedience salvation and stated that He does not break His promise, the promise does not restrict the absolute nature of His power (إطلاق قدرته). He remains, despite that, Powerful over what He wills, and His will is effective. Thus, there is no "security" in the sense of the negation of the power to do what contradicts the promise; therefore, fear remains in its place. For this reason, we see that He, the Exalted, says regarding His angels: **يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ** **"They fear their Lord [who is] above them, and they do what they are commanded"** (16:50), describing them with fear while He explicitly declares their infallibility (عصمتهم). He says regarding His prophets: **وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ** **"and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah"** (33:39). And He describes the believers in this verse with fear (الإشفاق) while He promises them at the end of the verses with a decisive word, saying: **أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ** **"They will be in gardens, held in high honor"** (70:35).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّهِمْ غَيْرُ مَأْمُونٍ** **"—indeed the punishment of their Lord is not something to be felt safe from—"** (70:28). This is a justification for their fear of the punishment of their Lord; it clarifies that they are correct in their fear of the punishment, and the reason for this has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حافِظُونَ** — until His saying — **هُمُ العادُونَ** **"and those who guard their private parts, (29) except from their spouses or their slave women, for then they are not blameworthy; (30) but whoever seeks beyond that—it is they who are the transgressors"** (70:29-31). The commentary on these three verses preceded at the beginning of Surah al-Mu’minun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ راعُونَ** **"and those who keep their trusts and their covenants"** (70:32). What is immediately understood from "trusts" (الأمانات) are the various types of trust with which they are entrusted, such as wealth and other things bequeathed regarding a person or honor. Their "observance" (رعايتهم) of them is that they protect them and do not betray them. It is said: because of the many types of trusts, the plural form was used, unlike "covenant" (العهد).

It is also said: what is meant by them is everything Allah has charged them with of belief and action; thus, it encompasses the rights of Allah and the rights of people. If they were to neglect any of them, they would have betrayed it.

It is also said: every blessing Allah has given His servant, such as limbs and other things, is a trust (أمانة). Whoever uses any of them for other than what Allah gave it for and permitted its use in, has betrayed it.

The apparent meaning of "covenant" (العهد) is a person's contract with another, by word or deed, regarding a matter. Its "observance" is to keep it and not break it without a justifying cause.

It is also said: the covenant is everything a person commits to for another. Thus, the servant's faith in his Lord is a covenant from him by which he covenanted his Lord to obey Him in all He charged him with. If he were to disobey Him in anything He commanded or forbade, he would have broken his covenant.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ بِشَهاداتِهِمْ قائِمُونَ** **"and those who are upright in their testimonies"** (70:33). Testimony (الشهادة) is well-known. "Standing by the testimony" (القيام بالشهادة) is the lack of refusal to bear it and the performance of what was borne exactly as it was borne, without concealment or alteration. The verses on this meaning are numerous.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ** **"and those who are watchful of their prayers"** (70:34). What is meant by "watchfulness" (المحافظة) over the prayer is observing its qualities of perfection according to what the Law (الشرع) has exhorted.

It is said: watchfulness over the prayer is different from constancy (الدوام) in it. Constancy relates to the prayer itself, while watchfulness relates to its quality. Thus, there is no repetition in mentioning watchfulness over it after mentioning constancy in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ مُكْرَمُونَ** **"They will be in gardens, held in high honor"** (70:35). The indication is to the "prayerful" in His saying: **إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ** (except the prayerful). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "gardens" (جنات) is for magnification (التفخيم). "In gardens" (فِي جَنَّاتٍ) is the predicate (خبر), and "held in high honor" (مُكْرَمُونَ) is a second predicate, or the phrase "in gardens" is an adverbial (ظرف) for His saying "held in high honor."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding **إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزُوعاً** (impatient when ill befalls him), he said: "Evil (الشر) is poverty and destitution." Regarding **وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الْخَيْرُ مَنُوعاً** (and grudging when good befalls him), he said: "Wealth and abundance."

In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), he said: "Then He made an exception and said: **إِلَّا الْمُصَلِّينَ** (except the prayerful), describing them by the best of their deeds: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ** (those who are persevering in their prayers). He says: If he makes something of the supererogatory prayers (النوافل) obligatory upon himself, he persists in it."

**I say:** His saying "If he makes... obligatory upon himself" etc.—the Imam (peace be upon him) derived this meaning from the annexation of prayer to the pronoun "their" (هُمْ), and we have pointed to this previously.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ** (and those who are watchful of their prayers). He said: "It is the obligatory prayer (الفريضة)." I said: **الَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمُونَ** (those who are persevering in their prayers). He said: "It is the supererogatory prayer (النافلة)."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ فِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلُومٌ** (and those in whose wealth there is a known right). It is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: "The known right is not from the *zakat*—it is something you take out from your wealth, if you wish every Friday, and if you wish every day; and for every possessor of merit is his merit."

He said: It is also narrated from him that he said: "It is that you maintain ties with kinship, give to the one who deprived you, and give charity to the one who showed enmity toward you."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in *al-Kafi* from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) through several paths, and it was narrated in *al-Mahasin* from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Safwan al-Jammal from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ** (for the beggar and the deprived). He said: "The deprived (المحروم) is the one whose livelihood is constrained (المحارف)—the one who has been deprived of the earning of his right hand in buying and selling."

He said: In another narration from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), they said: "The deprived is the man whose intellect has no defect, but his provision has not been expanded for him, and he is one whose livelihood is constrained (محارف)."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ هُمْ عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظُونَ** (and those who are watchful of their prayers). Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl narrated from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) that he said: "Those are the performers of the fifty prayers from among our followers (شيعتنا)."

**I say:** Perhaps this is based on what has been narrated from them (peace be upon them) that the legislation of the daily supererogatory prayers is to complete the obligatory ones.
